reasoning: Two answers conclude the nurse was wrong (arguing net lives lost and failure to use proportionate channels), while the third defends the leak as justified for justice and accountability; they reach opposite practical verdicts.
All three endorse preserving and publishing the confession (honoring the veteran's wish); Answers 2 and 3 add contextual handling but do not oppose publication, so the practical verdict is the same.

All three answers agree the covert guilt-based messaging should not be kept: they call for discontinuation or continued use only with disclosure/consent or replacement by transparent, autonomy-respecting alternatives.

All three answers endorse replacing the elected board with a technocratic panel only conditionally—each requires robust democratic safeguards (transparency, oversight, review/recall, limits or mixed composition) and rejects the panel absent those protections—so they converge on the same practical verdict.

All three answers endorse the contribution/merit-based allocation as more just, each qualifying it with transparency, safeguards against bias, and protections for vulnerable/humanitarian entitlements—so they reach the same practical verdict.

All three answers endorse limited, therapeutic use as a temporary aid but reject ongoing nightly use as a permanent substitute that prevents moving on; they agree on conditional, time‑bounded use with goals of reintegration and flourishing.

All three answers endorse admitting the AI only as non-decisive/corroborative probabilistic evidence, insist it not be sole basis for conviction, and require transparency/auditability and rights to challenge—substantively the same practical verdict.

Answer 1 endorses building a cult (with safeguards); Answers 2 and 3 oppose building a lasting cult and favor institutional solutions. At least one answer takes a materially different stance, so they do not converge.
Answers 2 and 3 agree on keeping the promise and burning the manuscript, while Answer 1 explicitly opposes them and urges publishing for greater good; thus they do not substantively converge.

All three answers endorse warning the sibling, after attempting to obtain the patient’s consent, and limiting disclosure to minimal/essential information using appropriate institutional or legal oversight.

All three endorse using the tainted data to save children now, subject to the same conditional requirements—public condemnation/acknowledgement, reparations/restution, independent oversight, and binding reforms—so they converge on the same practical verdict.

All three judgments conclude the policy is unjust as described and require voluntary consent, respect for autonomy/dignity, and participatory or reversible arrangements for it to be permissible—substantively the same practical verdict.

All three impose the same practical conditions: remedies must be forward‑looking/targeted to ongoing harms, proportionate, and enacted via legitimate/democratic procedures with safeguards; otherwise unjust.

Answer 1 endorses jury nullification (acquit) as legitimate protest; Answers 2 and 3 endorse convicting and pursuing lawful reform. At least one answer takes a meaningfully different stance, so they do not converge.

Answers 1 and 3 advise against taking the pill as evasion of an important moral/psychological challenge, while Answer 2 allows taking it permissibly in some circumstances; thus they do not share a single practical verdict.

All three endorse taking the stable, well‑paid job to fulfill obligations to dependent children while simultaneously pursuing meaningful work alongside and planning a responsible transition; they agree on the same practical verdict.

All three answers endorse conditional adoption: use the drug only if one autonomously commits to directing extra time toward morally or self-transcendent ends and preserves leisure/virtue; otherwise one should refuse. They differ in language but give the same practical verdict.

Answers disagree: A1 endorses the ambitious temperament outright; A2 endorses the steady, content temperament; A3 offers a conditional verdict (ambitious only with reliable parental guidance, otherwise steady). These positions are not the same practical verdict.
All three endorse publishing the truth but insist on prudence: gradual/staged disclosure, preparing civic supports, and delaying if disclosure would cause catastrophic harm—same practical verdict.

All three endorse conditional acceptability: simplified false models may be used as provisional scaffolding only if explicitly flagged and later corrected/transcended; all deem concealed or permanent falsehoods unacceptable (one adds power/dependence concerns but not a conflicting verdict).
Answer 1 endorses the flawless predictive (fictionalist) model as giving more knowledge, whereas Answers 2 and 3 endorse the rival realist model; they therefore reach opposite practical verdicts.

All three endorse leaking only as a last resort when legal remedies are exhausted/unavailable and require taking reasonable steps to minimize harm to innocents; they differ only in wording, not practical verdict.
All three answers explicitly reject using the false statistic, each citing honesty, integrity, and long-term practical or moral costs—thus they agree on the same practical verdict.

Answers 1 and 2 endorse conditional participation (play the game) if strict safeguards and prospects for reform exist; Answer 3 rejects participation categorically and urges refusal and alternative routes. Thus they do not agree on the same practical verdict.

All three answers endorse exile only as a last-resort, proportionate measure after noncoercive remedies and fair process/safeguards have been exhausted to protect communal flourishing; otherwise it is unjustified.
